name: HNMS Dolfijn (P 47)
events: The wartime history of HrMs Dolfijn as compiled on this page is extracted from this submarine's patrol reports and logbooks and was created in July 2016. Corrections and details regarding information from the enemy's side (for instance the composition of convoys attacked) is kindly provided by Mr. Platon Alexiades, a naval researcher from Canada.
This page was last updated in June 2026.

7 Oct 1942 Around 0915 A/7, Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) departed her builders yard at Barrow for Holy Loch. She was escorted by HrMs Jan van Gelder (Lt. P.L.M. van Geen, RNN). (1)
8 Oct 1942 Around 0445 A/8, Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) and HrMs Jan van Gelder (Lt. P.L.M. van Geen, RNN) arrived at Holy Loch from Barrow in Furness.

Dolfijn was placed in full commission (taken over from the shipyard) at Holy Loch later in the morning on completion of the acceptance trials. She was then to start a period of trials and training in Clyde area. (2)
10 Oct 1942 HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) conduced exercises in Loch Long. (3)
15 Oct 1942 HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) conduced exercises in the Clyde area. These included a deep dive trial in Inchmarnock Water. (3)
16 Oct 1942 HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) conduced exercises in the Clyde area. (3)
17 Oct 1942 HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) noise trials in Loch Goil. (3)
19 Oct 1942 HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) conducted full speed trials on the Arran measured mile. Also the log was calibrated. (3)
20 Oct 1942 HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) participated in A/S exercises off Campbeltown. (3)
21 Oct 1942 HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) participated in A/S exercises off Campbeltown. (3)
22 Oct 1942 HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) participated in A/S exercises off Campbeltown. (3)
23 Oct 1942 HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) proceeded from Campbeltown to Holy Loch. (3)
25 Oct 1942 HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) conducted torpedo discharge trials off Arrochar. (3)
26 Oct 1942 HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) conducted torpedo discharge trials off Arrochar. (3)
27 Oct 1942 HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) conducted torpedo discharge trials off Arrochar. (3)
29 Oct 1942 HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) conducted gunnery trials / exercises followed by trials / exercises with the mine detection unit (S.T.U.). (3)

30 Oct 1942 HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) conducted attack exercises in the Clyde area during which HMS Breda (Capt.(Retd.) A. E. Johnston, RN), HMS Z 5 (T/A/Lt.Cdr. S.T. Wenlock, RNR) and two MA/SB's acted as target.
HrMs Dolfijn also carried out gunnery exercises. (3)

30 Oct 1942 HMS P 216 (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN) conducted noise trials in Loch Goil. These were followed by night exercises in the Clyde area with HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN). (4)

31 Oct 1942 HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) conducted attack exercises in the Clyde area during which HMS Breda (Capt.(Retd.) A. E. Johnston, RN) and HMS Z 5 (T/A/Lt.Cdr. S.T. Wenlock, RNR) acted as target. (3)
1 Nov 1942 HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) conducted D/F trials.
Later HrMs Dolfijn conducted attack exercises in the Clyde area during which HMS Breda (Capt.(Retd.) A. E. Johnston, RN) and HMS Z 5 (T/A/Lt.Cdr. S.T. Wenlock, RNR) acted as target. (3)
3 Nov 1942 HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) conducted radar trials with aircraft and HrMs Jan van Gelder (Lt. P.L.M. van Geen, RNN). (3)

10 Nov 1942 HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) conducted attack exercises in the Clyde area during which HMS Breda (Capt.(Retd.) A. E. Johnston, RN) and HrMs Jan van Gelder (Lt. P.L.M. van Geen, RNN) acted as targets. (3)
11 Nov 1942 HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) conducted noise trials in Loch Goil. (3)
15 Nov 1942 Around 1920 A/15, HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) departed Holy Loch for her 1st war patrol. She was ordered to patrol in the Bay of Biscay in zone 'K 505'.

Passage south through the Irish Sea was made together with HMS Sealion (Lt. D. Lambert, DSC, RN). They were escorted until the vicinity of Bishops Rock by HMS Scimitar (Lt.Cdr. C.G. Cuthbertson, DSC, RNR) until 1900 A/17.

After parting company with the escort HrMs Dolfijn was to proceed to her patrol area through the following positions; 'A' = 49°55'N, 07°32'W. 'B' = 49°18'N, 12°25'W. 'D' = 44°28'N, 12°25'W at 2100 A/21. 'F' = 44°08'N, 08°58'W at 0030 A/23. (5)
15 Nov 1942 For the daily and attack positions of HrMs Dolfijn during her 1st war patrol see the map below.

26 Nov 1942 HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) attacked the German submarine U-263 with three torpedoes in position 43°31'N, 08°34'W. U-263 reported that two torpedoes ran underneath her. U-263 had been damaged by Allied escort vessels on 20 November forcing her to break off the patrol and was further damaged, rendering her unable to dive, by Allied aircraft on 24 November.
0750 A/26 - Asdic reported HE bearing 240°. Nothing could be seen through the periscope as it was still too dark for seeing any distance. Lights of fishing vessels were visible all round.
0758 A/26 - The Officer of the Watch sighted a small vessel, unidentified, and warned the Commanding Officer.
0800 A/26 - The Commanding Officer sighted a small darkened craft coming straight towards P 47. Changed course to get clear. Made ready for a torpedo attack.
0803 A/26 - Identified the target as a submarine at a range of 800 yards. Started attack.
0810 A/26 - Fired three torpedoes (four were intended). All missed. (6)
30 Nov 1942 The German tanker Antarktis (10711 GRT, built 1939) left Vigo at 0030 A/30 while the Italian blockade breaker Cortellazzo (5292 GRT, built 1931) was on passage from Bordeaux to Japan.
The Cortellazo was detected at 0400 A/30, by an Allied aircraft in position 44°30'N, 09°18'W, course 230°, speed 10 knots.

The submarines HMS Clyde (Lt. R.S. Brookes, DSC, RN), HMS Sealion (Lt. D. Lambert, DSC, RN), HMS Graph (Lt. P.B. Marriott, RN) and HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) were patrolling / on passage in the general area.

In a signal timed 0541 A/30, HMS Sealion reported the following to the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.); Unknown ship in position 44°52'N, 10°32'W, course 270°, speed unknown. 82 round per minute were counted. Enemy vessel probably diesel driven [which was correct]. Red Very lights only seen.
At 0654 A/30, another aircraft reported one unidentified motor vessel in position 43°58'N, 09°59'W, course 270°, speed 15 knots.
In a signal timed 0715 A/30, the F.O.S. sent the following to HMS Graph; Total bombing restrictions arranged. If nothing sighted by 1400 A/30 double on your tracks to zone 'K 501'.
At 0725 A/30, an aircraft reported that it had attacked the enemy motor vessel by level bombing. The results were unobserved.
The same aircraft reported at 0950 A/30 that the enemy had been in position 44°25'N, 11°10'W at 0850 A/30. Enemy course was 270°, speed 12 knots.

In a signal timed 1034 A/30, the F.O.S. sent the following orders to HMS Clyde and HMS Graph. Estimated position, course and speed of the enemy motor vessel at 0850 A/30, 44°40'N. 11°10'W, 260°, 13 knots. Proceeding forthwith on surface to intercept.
In a signal timed 1241 A/30, the F.O.S. sent the following orders to the submarines; German tanker Antarktis left Vigo at 0030 A/30. HrMs Dolfijn patrol in the vicinity of Cape Ortegal.

In a signal timed 1334 A/30 the F.O.S. sent the following to the submarines; Estimate Antarktis will pass Vallarno between 1100 A/30 and 1200 A/30. Endeavour intercept as far to westward as practicable. At all costs avoid being seen.

In a signal timed 1404 A/30, the F.O.S. sent the following to the submarines; 1) Liberator aircraft diverted from patrol B.P. 16 to locate and shadow the Cortellazzo. 2) A second Liberator proceeding to position 43°45'N, 07°40'W, thence coastwise westwards to locate the Antarktis.
In a signal timed 1531 A/30, the C-in-C Plymouth, reported the following to the F.O.S.; Cortellazzo in position 44°26'N, 13°38'W, course 260° to 270°, speed 13 knots, at 1415 A/30.
In a signal timed 1547 A/30, the F.O.S. informed the submarines of the following; My signal timed 1334 A/30 refers to HrMs Dolfijn only.

In a signal timed 1606 A/30, the F.O.S. informed the submarines of the following; Aircraft reported the approximate position, course and speed of the Cortellazzo at 1415 A/30 as 44°36'N, 13°38'E, 260°, 13 knots.
At 1614 A/30, an aircraft reported the following; Have attacked enemy ship at bomb level. Estimate no hits. Position of enemy at 1540 A/30 was 44°46'N, 12°37'W.

In a signal timed 1725 A/30, HMS Clyde reported the following to the F.O.S.; Your 1606 A/30. My position, course and speed are 44°38'N, 14°10'E, 210°, 13 knots. Estimate enemy passing 20 miles ahead. Am searching to westward till dark then will return to original route. Estimate fuel remaining on arrival at Bishop Rock on 4 December 12 hours steaming.

In a signal timed 1832 A/30, the F.O.S. sent the following to HMS Clyde and HMS Graph; l) Estimate enemy furthest on longitude at dawn first December will be 18°45'W. 2) If not in contact with enemy and fuel permits proceed with dispatch towards positions as follows and establish patrol as follows; HMS Clyde, 44°01'N, 18°40'W, HMS Graph, 43°35'n, 18°40'W. 3) Total bombing restrictions extended.

In a signal timed 1952 A/50 the F.O.S. informed HrMs Dolfijn of the following; Estimate the Antarktis can cross 05°30'W at 0730 A/1. If not in contact proceed eastwards at your best speed keeping close outside Spanish territorial waters. Endeavour to remain on the surface until 0830 A/1 but it is essential you are not sighted. Enemy air activity is likely along the route of the Antarktis.

In a signal timed 2026 A/30, the F.O.S. sent the following to HMS Clyde; Your 1725 A/30. 1) My 1832 A/30. Cancel instructions to Clyde. 2) Clyde proceed dived by day by route in my signal timed 1059 A/26, passing through position 46°01'N, 13°22'W at 0800 A/1. (3) Bombing restrictions will follow.

In a signal timed 2341 A/30 the F.O.S. sent the following orders to HrMs Dolfijn; My 1952 A/30. Amend as follows. Second paragraph proceed at best speed along line 070° from 43°51'N, 07°53'W. Cancel the third paragraph. Continue on the surface as long as practicable. (7)

1 Dec 1942 In a signal timed 1556 A/1, the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) sent the following orders to HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN); Establish a patrol on line 070° - 250° in the vicinity of position 44°45'N, 03°30'W. Further orders will be forwarded later.

In a signal timed 1938 A/1, the F.O.S. sent the following orders to HrMs Dolfijn; Leave patrol after dark/2. proceed submerged by day through position 'X' (46°01'N, 03°59'W), 'Y' (47°59'N, 06°50'W) at 0800 A/4 to make rendezvous in position 'Z' (180° - Lizard Head - 10 miles) at 0800 A/5 as ordered by the Capt. S.3. Bombing restrictions will follow. (8)
2 Dec 1942 Around 1200 A/2, HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) left patrol to return to the U.K. (9)
5 Dec 1942 Around 1445 A/5, HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) ended her 1st war patrol at Falmouth.
She was escorted in [name of the escort not known to us at the moment]. The escort had joined around 1215 A/5. (9)

6 Dec 1942 Around 0100 A/6, HMS Sealion (Lt. D. Lambert, DSC, RN) and HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) departed Falmouth for Holy Loch. They were escorted by HMS White Bear (Cdr.(Retd.) C.C. Flemming, RN).
Around 0800 A/6, near Bishops Rock, HMS Graph (Lt. P.B. Marriott, RN) joined company. (10)
8 Dec 1942 Around 1530 A/8, HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) arrived at Holy Loch. (9)

22 Dec 1942 Around 1300 A/22, HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) departed Holy Loch for the passage to Gibraltar. En-route Dolfijn was ordered to patrol in the Bay of Biscay to intercept the German bockade runner Rhakotis making this passage her 2nd war patrol.
During her passage south through the Irish Sea she was escorted by HMS St. Elstan (Lt. R.M. Roberts, RNR).
HrMs Dolfijn was to part company with the escort in position 000° - Round Island (Scilly Isles) - 7 miles at 1500 A/24.
Thence through position 50°10'N, 09°01'W, followed by route 'Blue' to 43°00'N. Further routing would be signalled by the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic (F.O.C.N.A.). (11)
22 Dec 1942 For the daily positions of HrMs Dolfijn during her 2nd war patrol see the map below.

24 Dec 1942 In a signal timed 1255 A/24, HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) informed the Capt. S.3 of the following; Have two sailors seriously ill, possibly appendicitis. Weather unsuitable for transfer at sea. Request authority to proceed with escort to Falmouth after meeting USS Gurnard (T/Cdr. C.H. Andrews, USN).
Around 1600 A/24, USS Gurnard joined HrMs Dolfijn and HMS St. Elstan (Lt. R.M. Roberts, RNR) in the ordered rendezvous position. Course was set to proceed to Falmouth.
Around 2315 A/24, HrMs Dolfijn, USS Gurnard and HMS St. Elstan put into Falmouth. HrMs Dolfijn landed her two sick ratings. One of which was operated on immediately for appendicitis. (9)

27 Dec 1942 After taking on board three ratings that had to come from Dundee, HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN), departed Falmouth around 0930 A/27 to continue her passage to Gibraltar. She was escorted out (until 1740 A/27) by HMS Lord Inchcape (T/Lt. A.C. Baxter, RNVR) and HMS Conway Castle (T/Lt. N.G. Nell, RNVR).

They were to pass through position 180° - Lizard Head - 3 miles, thence through 180° - Bishop Rock - 10 miles westwards until dusk, thence submarine to the westward and the trawlers returning to Falmouth.
HrMs Dolfijn was to pass through position 43°01'N, 13°50'E at 0001 A/2. (11)

30 Dec 1942 In a signal timed 1735 A/30 the Vice Admiral Gibraltar sent the following orders to HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN); 1)After crossing 43°01'N, at 0001 A/2 proceed on the surface at the Commanding Officers discretion through the following positions; 'A' = 36°55'N, 09°11'W at 1000 A/4. 'B' = 36°37'N, 07°26'W at 2200 A/4. 'C' = 35°54'N, 06°15'W at 0800 A/5. Thence to Gibraltar. 2) Total bombing restrictions arranged 10 miles each side of route 35 miles ahead and astern of your estimated position report if unable keep within bombing restriction. 3) You should not pass position 'C' before 0800 A/5. (8)

31 Dec 1942 HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) was ordered to intercept the German blockade runner Rhakotis (6753 GRT, built 1928) (Admiral Submarine's (F.O.S.) signal timed 1512 A/31).
HrMs Dolfijn was to patrol in position 45°49'N, 12°22'W as soon as possible after dawn on 1 January. (9)

1 Jan 1943 During the night, HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) sighted flares dropped by aircraft but is unable to intercept the German blockade breaker Rhakotis (6753 GRT, built 1928).
In the evening Dolfijn is ordered to continue her passage to Gibraltar. The German blockade breaker had meanwhile been intercepted and sunk. (9)

1 Jan 1943 In a signal timed 0656 A/1, the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) informed USS Shad (T/Cdr. E.J. MacGregor, 3rd, USN), USS Blackfish (T/Cdr. J.F. Davidson, USN), USS Herring (T/Cdr. R.W. Johnson, USN), USS Barb (T/Cdr. J.R. Waterman, USN) and HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) of the following; Aircraft report one enemy merchant ship within 15 miles of position 45°45'N, 13°50'W, course 90°, speed 11 knots. Inward-bound blockade-runners are sometimes escorted by U-Boats.

In a signal timed 0719 A/1 the F.O.S. sent the following orders to the above listed submarines; My 0641 A/1. If not acting on aircraft homing signals submarines operate as follows; HrMs Dolfijn between parallels 46°20'N and 46°01'N. USS Blackfish between 46°01'N and 45°40'N. USS Herring between 45°40'N and 45°20'N. HMS Barb between 45°20'N and 45°01'N. All submarines making west as far as possible to ensure interception of the enemy in daylight.

In a signal timed 0909 A/1 the F.O.S. sent the following information to the submarines listed above; Estimated positions of our surface forces at 0800/1; 1) HMS Scylla (Capt. I.A.P. Macintyre, CBE, DSO, RN) in position 42°50'N, 16°01'W. Northbound convoy MKS 4 escorted by sloops and corvettes in position 45°40'N, 14°50'W. 2) HMS Scylla and three escorts of MKS 4 have been ordered to proceed to intercept and have been informed our submarines are expected to intercept east of 13°00'W. [The Senior Officer of the escort had detached two of the escorts instead of three, this were HMS Lulworth (Lt.Cdr.(Retd.) C. Gwinner, DSO, RN) and HMCS Moosejaw (T/Lt. L.D. Quick, RCN).]

In a signal timed 1114 A/1, the F.O.S. sent the following information the the submarines listed above; My 0909 A/1, Paragraph 2, position, course and speed of HMS Lulworth and HMCS Moosejaw is 45° 37'N, 14°01'W, 090°, 15 knots.

In a signal timed 1151 A/1, the following orders were sent to the submarines listed; Estimate longitude enemy at 1200 A/1 was 12°30'W. If to westward of enemy USS Blackfish, USS Herring and USS Barb sweep on course 90° at best convenient speed.

In a signal timed 1247 A/1, the F.O.S. sent the following orders to the submarines listed; My 1151 A/1. USS Blackfish, USS Herring and USS Barb are to operate between limits of latitudes ordered in my signal timed 0719 A/1. Adjust course as necessary.

In a signal timed 1624 A/1, the following was sent by the F.O.S.; 1) Estimate Dolfijn's position at 2000 A/1 as being 46°05'N, 12°30'W. 2) Dolfijn is to proceed forthwith on route 'Red', diving by day, passing through position 'C' (42°59'N, 12°26'W at 0600 A/3.

In a signal timed 1714 A/1, the following information and orders were sent by the F.O.S.; Aircraft report position, course and speed of the enemy at 1634 A/1 as being 45°12'N, 10°48'W, 090°, 12 knots. USS Shad and HrMs Dolfijn proceed to intercept.
In a signal timed 1830 A/1 to the Admiralty, HMS Scylla reported to have sunk the inbound enemy blockade breaker.

In a signal timed 1923 A/1, the F.O.S. sent the following information / orders; 1) Enemy was sunk by HMS Scylla at 1830 A/1. 2) Submarines return forthwith to original patrol. USS Shad off Bilbao, USS Barb off Vigo, USS Blackfish in zone 'K 501' and USS Herring off Ferrol. (12)

2 Jan 1943 In a signal timed 0114 A/2 the Vice Admiral Gibraltar, sent the following orders to HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN); Cancel paragraphs 1 and 3 of my signal timed 1735 A/30. After crossing 42°59'N at 0600 A/3 proceed on the surface at the Commanding Officers discretion through the following positions; 'A' = 36°55'N, 09°11'W at 1000 A/5. 'B' = 36°37'N, 07°26'W at 2200 A/5th. 'C' = 35°54'N, 06°15'W at 0800 A/6th, thence to Gibraltar. Speed of advance 7.5 knots. You should not pass position 'C' before 0800 A/6 (13)
6 Jan 1943 Around 1400 A/6, HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) ended her 2nd war patrol at Gibraltar. (9)

13 Jan 1943 After two days of exercises out of Gibraltar, HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) departed Gibraltar for her 3rd war patrol (1st in the Mediterranean) around 1430 A/13. On departure gunnery exercises were carried out. HrMs Dolfijn was ordered to patrol off Alicante, Spain in an area bounded by the Greenwich meridan, Latitude 38°00'N and the coast of Spain.

She was routed through the following positions; 'P' = 180° - Cape Calbuerras - 5 miles. 'Q' = 180° - Cape Degata - 5 miles. 'R' = 135° - Cape Palos - 5 miles. 'S' = 38°00'N, 00°28'W at 2200 A/15. Speed of advance was estimated to be 120 miles per day.
For the daily positions of HrMs Dolfijn during this patrol see the map below. The map is however incomplete. No positions are currently known for 16 to 20 January 1943.
16 Jan 1943 At 1540 A/16, HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) carried out a dummy attack on a Spanish merchant vessel which was estimated as being around 3000 tons. (9)
18 Jan 1943 In a signal timed 1340 A/18, the Capt. S.8 sent the following to HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN); You will be ordered to leave patrol P.M./21 for Algiers. (13)
18 Jan 1943 At 1600 A/18, HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) carried out a dummy attack on a Spanish merchant vessel. (9)

20 Jan 1943 In a signal timed 1534 A/20, the Capt. S.8 sent the following orders to HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN); Leave patrol passing through position 38°26'N, 00°01'E at 2000 A/21 and 38°26'N, 02°30'E at 2000 A/22. Thence on the surface at the Commanding Officers discretion. Make rendezvous with the escort in position 37°01'N, 02°59'E at 0801 A/23 for onward escort to Algiers. 2. All positions North and East. (Capt. S.8, 1534 A/20 to DOLFIJN, (13)

20 Jan 1943 At 2148 A/20, HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) carried out a dummy attack on a Spanish merchant vessel. The attack was carried out from submerged in brilliant moonlight. (9)
21 Jan 1943 Around 2035 A/21, in position 38°26'N, 00°01'E, HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) left patrol to proceed to Algiers. (9)
21 Jan 1943 At 1500 A/21, HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) carried out a dummy attack on the Spanish merchant vessel Isla de Tenerife (5334 GRT, built 1921).
Around 2035 A/21, in position 38°26'N, 00°01'E, HrMs Dolfijn left patrol to proceed to Algiers. (9)
23 Jan 1943 HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) ended her 3rd war patrol (1st in the Mediterranean) at Algiers.
She was escorted in (escort not identified). The escort had joined around 0800 A/23. (9)

1 Feb 1943 Around 1730 A/1, HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) departed Algiers for her 4th war patrol (2nd in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol to the west and south of Sardinia in an area bounded by lat. 38°40'N and 41°45'N and by long. 07°30'E and 09°10'E. The patrol was considered to be a working-up patrol in an area where enemy A/S activity was expected to be not intense.
HrMs Dolfijn was routed through positions 38°17'N, 04°04'E and 39°02'N, 07°30'E.
For the daily and attack positions of HrMs Dolfijn during this patrol see the map below. The map is however incomplete. No daily positions are currently known for 4 to 10 February 1943.
5 Feb 1943 In a signal timed 1832 A/5 to HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN), the Capt. S.8 issued the following orders; My 1824 A/5. Keep south of 41°00'N after 0600 A/9.
[The Capt. S.8's signal timed 1824 A/5 had contained patrol and passage orders for HMS P 247 (Lt. M.G.R. Lumby, DSC, RN). (15)

6 Feb 1943 At 1710 A/6, HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) sighted a small Italian patrol vessel armed with a machine gun. Displacement was estimated to be around 200 tons. The vessel came round Cape Moro Point, Corsica straight out of the sun. A gun action was considered but not carried through.

It was later, around 1845 A/6, intended to attack the vessel when it entered Propriano harbour but the enemy could no longer be seen in the fading light conditions. HrMs Dolfijn then set course to patrol off Sardinia.
[We have been unable to identify this vessel.] (9)

7 Feb 1943 In a signal timed 1704 A/7, the Capt. S.8 sent the following orders to HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN); Leave patrol passing through position 38°54'N, 08°10'E at 2200 A/10 and 38°12'N, 03°55'E at 2130 A/12 to make rendezvous with the escort for Algiers in position 37°00'N, 03°10'E at 0730 A/13. (15)

9 Feb 1943 The Italian submarine Malachite was torpedoed and sunk near Cape Spartivento, Sardinia, Italy in position 38°42'N, 08°52'E by the Dutch submarine HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN). Her commanding officer, T.V. Alpino Cinti, three officers and nine ratings were picked up by the auxiliary submarine chaser Arcioni and the fishing vessel Dori. One officer and thirty-four ratings were lost.
0740 A/9 - Started an attack on what turned out to be a Crotone-class minelayer but abandoned the attack as these ships had only a draught of 7 feet.
1047 A/9 - Heard HE bearing 100°.
1048 A/9 - Sighted a submarine bearing 095°. Started attack. The submarine was identified as Italian, the Ensign was clearly visible, most likely a Gemma-class boat.

1059 A/9 - Fired a full salvo of four torpedoes from 2200 yards. One hit was obtained. The enemy quickly started to sink by the stern. When almost gone the submarine popped up vertically out of the water with the bow visible almost up to the conning tower before she slipped under the waves for the final time. (9)
10 Feb 1943 Around 2359 A/10, HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) left patrol to return to Algiers. (9)
13 Feb 1943 Around 0820 A/13, HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) joined company with her escort into Algiers harbour, HMHDML 1249 (?). (16)

23 Feb 1943 Around 1800 A/23, HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) departed Algiers for her 5th war patrol (3rd in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol within 30 miles of the east coast Sardinia and between 38°45'N and 41°30'N.
HrMs Dolfijn was routed through positions 38°36'N, 04°46'E at 2100 A/24 and 38°45'N, 09°11'S at 2000 A/26.
HrMs Dolfijn was escorted out until midnight by two HDML's.
For the daily and attack positions of HrMs Dolfijn during this patrol see the map below. The map is however incomplete. No daily positions are currently known for 27 February to 7 March 1943.
26 Feb 1943 At 0830 A/26, HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) sighted smoke near Cape Teulada moving east. HrMs Dolfijn closed on course 360° at 5 knots.
At 0940 A/26, the origin of the smoke was identified as a Crotone-class minelayer. Broke off the attack at a range of 5 nautical miles.

[We have been unable to identify this vessel, note: the submarine Argento was exercising with the auxiliary submarine chaser Nuova Vincenzina in the Gulf of Cagliari (0821 B-1305 B/26) but this was further to the east].
At 1143 A/26, when to the south of Cape Spartivento a merchant vessel was sighted bearing 340° at a range of 3 nautical miles. Enemy course was 090°.
An attack was started but the range could not be closed sufficiently and the attack was broken off. The vessel was thought to be a modern diesel engined vessel, possibly a fruit carrier.
This was most probably the German Tell (former French, 1349 GRT, built 1938). She had sailed from Porto Vesme at 1000 B/26 and arrived at Cagliari at 1600 B/26. (9)

28 Feb 1943 At 1505 A/28, HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) sighted what is thought to be a German submarine coming round Cavoli Island on a course of 070°. Started an attack. Range was about 3.5 nautical miles. Enemy speed was estimated at 12 knots.
The enemy was zigzagging erratically with a lot of course and speed changes and the result was that an attack was not possible.
This was most likely the Italian submarine Wolframio which had departed Cagliari around 1223 B/28 for La Maddalena where she arrived around 0914 A/1. (9)

1 Mar 1943 HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) attacked an Italian submarine with four torpedoes off Cavoli Island. No hits were obtained. This was the Argento on passage from Cagliari to La Maddalena. Dolfijn was later hunted by the auxiliary patrol boats Idria and Bella Italia.
In a signal timed 1940 A/1 the Capt. S.8 informed HrMs Dolfijn of the following; At 1400 hours, you may have been reported, there is an enemy submarine in your vicinity.
1300 A/1 - Sighted an Italian Gemma-class or Sirena-class submarine coming round Cavoli Island. Closed to attack.
1312 A/1 - Fired a salvo of four torpedoes from 3500 yards. All missed ahead.
1316 A/1 - Sighted a twin-engine aircraft diving steeply and turning towards Dolfijn. Went to 120 feet.
1318 A/1 - Three explosions. One was rather near but it caused no damage.
2 Mar 1943 At 0915 A/2, HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) sighted an A/S schooner entering Cagliari Bay. No attack was made.

3 Mar 1943 At 0845 A/3, HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) sighted the smoke of two vessels leaving Cagliari Bay. These were later identified as two Crotone-class minelayers following the swept channel.
[We have been unable to identify these vessels.]

At 1540 A/3, a small floating crane towed by two small tugs and escorted by a small type of motor torpedo boat passed proceeding towards Cagliari. Their draught was insufficient to make a torpedo attack and the proximity of an enemy airfield and the motor torpedo boat made a gun action too risky.
These were the tugs Salvore and Nereo, the latter towing a pontoon, and escorted by MAS 502. They had sailed from Arbatax at 0400 B/3 and arrived at Cagliari at 1955 B/3. (9)
4 Mar 1943 At 1230 A/4, HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) sighted what is thought to be an RD-class minesweeper entering Cagliari Bay.

5 Mar 1943 At 1015 A/5, HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) sighted a small floating crane, towed by one tug and escorted by two armed trawlers, passing within one mile of Cape Spartivento, course west, thus passing well westward of the swept channel. This was a different crane then the one sighted on 3 March.
At 1353 A/5, HrMs Dolfijn started an attack on what was thought to be a small tanker coming from Cagliari along the swept channel.
At 1407 A/5, the ship however turned round and returned to Cagliari. It was now seen to be a patrol trawler. (9)

6 Mar 1943 At 0900 A/6, while patrolling west of Cavoli Island, HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) sighted two A/S trawlers and a 'Crotone-class' minelayer closing. The minelayer passed overhead but HrMs Dolfijn was not detected.
[We have been unable to identify these vessels.] (9)

6 Mar 1943 In a signal timed 1552 A/6, the Capt. S.8 sent the following orders to HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN); Leave patrol so as to pass through positions 38°40'N, 09°01'E at 1959 A/8 and 38°04'N, 04°25'E at 2001 A/10, thence proceed on surface at discretion to make rendezvous with the escort to Algiers in position 36°57'N, 03°13'E at 0700 A/11.

In a signal timed 1358 A/7 the Capt. S.8 adjusted the rendezvous with the escort to Algiers to position 36°58'N, 03°03'E at 0800 A/11. HMS Unshaken (Lt. J. Whitton, RN) was given the same rendezvous position and time. (17)

7 Mar 1943 Between 0900 A/7 and 1100 A/7, while patrolling near Cape Spartivento, HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) sighted two minesweeping trawlers, a sailing schooner and some fishing vessels.
At 1130 A/7, an RD-class minesweeper was sighted coming from Cagliari. She turned back at 1150 A/7.
[We have been unable to identify all these vessels.] (9)
8 Mar 1943 HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) patrolled off Cape Spartivento the entire day. Only one vessel was sighted, an A/S schooner at 1540 A/8.
On surfacing at 0833 A/8, HrMs Dolfijn left patrol to return to Algiers. (9)

10 Mar 1943 In a signal timed 1300 A/10, the Capt. S.10 informed HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) and HMS Unshaken (Lt. J. Whitton, RN) of the following; 1) My signal timed 1358 A/7 paragraph 3. Escort will be trawler HMS Cava (T/Lt. R.L. Petty-Major, RNVR). 2) Convoy due Algiers from the westward at 1200 A/11. 3) One trawler and one H.D.M.L. leaving Algiers bound eastwards at 0600 A/11. 4) Keep a good lookout for an enemy submarine near Algiers. (18)
11 Mar 1943 Around 1030 A/11, HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) ended her 5th war patrol (3rd in the Mediterranean) at Algiers.
She was escorted in by HMS Cava (T/Lt. R.L. Petty-Major, RNVR) which had joined around 0900 A/11. (9)
14 Mar 1943 HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) is docked in the small graving dock at Algiers. (19)
16 Mar 1943 HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) is undocked. (19)

23 Mar 1943 Around 1830 A/23, HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) departed Algiers for her 6th war patrol (4th in the Mediterranean). Once again she was ordered to patrol off Sardinia.
For the daily and attack positions of HrMs Dolfijn during this patrol see the map below. The map is however incomplete. No daily positions are currently known for 27 March to 5 April 1943.

28 Mar 1943 At 0835 A/28, north off Arbatax, Sardinia, HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) sighted a small merchant vessel coast crawling very close inshore. HrMs Dolfijn closed to try to identify the vessel but it passed out of range.
Around 1500 A/28, HrMs Dolfijn entered the Gulf of Tortoli to investigate Arbatax harbour. Four schooners were seen, one of them was a large one. Unfortunately seas were too rough for gun action.

28 Mar 1943 In a signal timed 1500 A/28 the Capt. S.8 ordered HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) to leave zone 'J' through position 39°20'N, 10°30'E at 0001 A/30th thence course 090° to zone 'D'. (20)

29 Mar 1943 HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) torpedoed and sank the Italian merchant vessel Egle (1143 GRT, built 1893) off Cavoli Island, Sardinia. She was travelling from Cagliari to La Maddalena. Six of the crew of the Egle were killed. [We have been unable to identify the escort.]
0840 A/29 - Sighted a merchant vessel of 4000-5000 tons escorted by one RD-class minesweeper. Started attack.
0912 A/29 - Fired a salvo of three torpedoes. Obtained one hit in the engine room.
0915 A/29 - The target had a starboard list of 20°.

0919 A/29 - Had another look through the periscope. The target was no longer sighted and must have gone down. The minesweeper however was quite near so went deep but fortunately no counter-attack developed.
Later that day, Dolfijn left patrol off Sardinia to patrol further to the eastwards. (9)
29 Mar 1943 In a signal timed 1156 A/29, HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) was ordered by the Capt. S.8 to patrol in zone 'E' after 0300 A/31. (20)
30 Mar 1943 At 1658 A/30, HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) received Capt. S 8's signal timed 1541 A/30.

By 1728 A/30, the signal was decoded and it contained information of an enemy convoy of six merchant vessels, three destroyers, two E-boats at 1420 A in position 39°40'N, 12°22'E, course 220, speed 10 knots.
At 1740 A/30, HrMs Dolfijn surfaced as it would only be possible to intercept the convoy by travelling at utmost speed on the surface.
At 1745 A/30, Capt. S 8's signal timed 1623 A/30 was decoded which gave the position of the enemy at 1610 A/30 as 39°33'N, 12°00'E, course 142°.

At 1800 A/30, HrMs Dolfijn was forced to dive for an approaching JU 88 aircraft which had obviously seen the submarine as she turned sharply towards. The attempt to intercept this convoy had now to be abandoned.

HrMs Dolfijn proceeded southwards in the hope more reconnaissance information would come in and it would still be possible to try to intercept the convoy further southwards but no more infomation came in and at 2140 A/30, HrMs Dolfijn shaped course to patrol near Ustica.

Three enemy convoys were at sea this day; 1) The Italian tranports Bivona (former French Socombel, 1646 GRT, built 1930), Giacomo C. (4638 GRT, built 1920) [at 2320 B/29, she signalled she could not keep up with the convoy and was ordered to Palermo escorted by Dezza] and Aquila (former French Algérie, 3386 GRT, built 1910), the German transport Charles Le Borgne (former French, 1426 GRT, built 1920). They were escorted by the Italian destroyer Lubiana, the Italian torpedo boats Giuseppe Dezza and Tifone and the German auxiliary submarine chasers UJ 2205 and UJ 2208 sailed from Naples between 1630 B-1800 B/29 proceeding to Bizerte at 7 knots.

2) The Italian transport Belluno (former French Fort de France, 4279 GRT, built 1935) and the German Pierre-Claude (formrr French, 1794 GRT, built 1934) escorted by the Italian torpedo boats Sagittario, Fortunale (escort leader), Antares and Enrico Cosenz sailed from Naples between 1605 B-1730 B/29 for Tunis via Trapani and Favignana.

3) The Italian transports Nuoro (former French Saint Ambroise, 7305 GRT, built 1920), Crema (former French Hébé, 1684 GRT, built 1920), Chieti (former French Arizona, 5457 GRT, built 1925) and Benevento (former French Fort de Souville, 5229 GRT, built 1918) escorted by the Italian torpedo boats Clio, Cigno (escort leader) and Cassiopea (joined at 1520 B/30 by the corvette Cicogna from Trapani which escorted the steamer Chieti to Palermo) and the German auxiliary submarine chasers UJ 2202 and UJ 2203 sailed from Naples/Pozzuoli/Baia at 1900-1915 B/29 for Bizerte. (9)

2 Apr 1943 At 2035 A/2, HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) received the Capt. S.8's signal timed 0215 A/2, in which she was ordered to operate at the Commanding Officers discretion in zones 'A' and 'B'.
At 2140 A/2, course was set to patrol off the Gulf of Castellammare. (9)
4 Apr 1943 At 0740 A/4, near Cape San Vito, HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) sighted a small tug proceeding eastwards keeping very close inshore.
At 1315 A/4, two F-boats and one other landing craft were seen proceeding westwards.
This may have been vessels from the following group which had sailed from Palermo at 0900 B/4 and reached Bizerte at 1220 B/5. MZ 777, MZ 787, MZ 788, MZ 789 and MZ 790.
At 1800 A/4, HMS Dolfijn proceeded northwards, retiring from the coast.
At 1915 A/4, HMS Dolfijn surfaced and set course to proceed to the Marettimo area.

At 2020 A/4, the Capt. S.8's signal timed 1216 A/4 was received which contained the following orders for her; Patrol to the west of Marittimo during the night of 5/6 April leaving patrol A.M./6 for Algiers. (9)

5 Apr 1943 At 1730 A/5, near Marettimo, HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) sighted a convoy of six landing barges. They came from the south, keeping close to the east coast of Marettimo and after rounding the northern corner they set course 240° apparently bound for Tunis. Their speed was about 5 knots. All appeared to be heavily armed so gun action was out of the question. Photographs were taken through the periscope.

The movement around Marettimo is very odd. Perhaps the vessels sighted were the German Siebel ferries SF 213, SF 86, SF 94, SF 214, SF 195, SF 183 together with I-B 60 and I-B 27 sailed from Marsala at 1400 B/5 April for Tunis (but why would they go around Marettimo ?, Weather ? diversion for Allied aircraft ? or ???). (see T1022/2560, PG 45411). They arrived at La Goulette at 1600 B/6. (21)

5 Apr 1943 In a signal timed 1224 A/5, the Capt. S.8 sent the following orders to HMS Sibyl (Lt. E.J.D. Turner, DSC, RN) and HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN); Try and be in position 237° - 67 miles from point 'F' by 0400 A/6 and 252° - 42 miles by 0130 A/6 respectively.

In a follow up signal timed 1238 A/5 the Capt. S.8 sent the following orders to the same submarines; My 1224 A/5, Dolfijn leaves patrol at 0400 A/6 through position 38°22'N, 10°50'E at 2150 A/6 thence course 270°, speed of advance 105 miles a day. After 0800 A/6, Sibyl has freedom of action in zones 'A' and 'B'. (22)

5 Apr 1943 In a signal timed 1600 A/5, the Capt. S.8 sent the following orders to HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN); Onward routeing through positions 38°22'N, 08°37'E at 2130 A/7, 37°57'N, 04°16'E at 2130 A/9 to make rendezvous with the escort to Algiers in position 36°59'N, 03°19'E at 0630 A/10. (22)

6 Apr 1943 At 0103 A/6, still patrolling in the Marettimo area, HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) received an enemy report from an aircraft, time 0155 B/6 [note the one hour difference of time zone] reporting an enemy convoy proceeding on a course of 240° about 18 nautical miles from Dolfijn's current position.
At 0115 A/6, an amplifying report was received, timed 0210 B/5, giving the number of escort vessels as five. [Note: the Italians intercepted these signals at 0130 B/6 at 0210 B/6.]

The convoy referred to was probably the one made up of the Italian transport Rovereto (former French Yalou, 8564 GRT, built 1914) and the German transports San Diego (former French, 6013 GRT, built 1930) and Carbet (former French, 3689 GRT, built 1920) escorted by the Italian torpedo boats Perseo (escort leader), Orione, Pallade, Clio, Libra and the Italian destroyer Augusto Riboty. They sailed from Naples between 0115 B-0330 B/5 for Bizerte, except Clio and Augusto Riboty who sailed from Messina at 0420 B/5 and joined at 1551 B/5. They came under submarine attack (bogus) at 1845 B/5, torpedo bomber attack at 1925 B/5 and bomber attack at 1100 B/6 without result. Rovereto and San Diego were finally sunk by air attack at 1725 B/6 by Liberator bombers just 5 miles from Bizerte. This convoy initially included the Italian transport Caserta (former French Sidi Okba, 2824 GRT, built 1929) but at 0355 B/5 she signalled that her steering gear was defective, and she was ordered to return to Pozzuoli.
At 0240 A/6, a hospital ship with correct markings was sighted passing three miles to the north of HrMs Dolfijn.
Probably this was the Toscana (9429 GRT, built 1923) which had sailed at 1500 B/5 from Tunis for Leghorn.

At 0405 A/6, a ship was heard on the Asdic. A small destroyer was subsequently sighted proceeding on course 240°. She was doing about 200 revs. Two minutes later HrMs Dolfijn dived to avoid being sighted.

The corvette Cicogna was at 0300 B/6 in position 38°02'N, 12°20'E [this should be further to the west then where Dolfijn was patrolling] steering for Trapani. However, she arrived at Trapani at 0712 B/6 which is rather odd as she did not needed four hours to reach her destination. Perhaps she conducted an A/S patrol in the area before proceeding to Trapini.
At 0415 A/6, faint HE was heard to the north-west.
At 0420 A/6, HrMs Dolfijn surfaced.
At 0432 A/6, two silhouettes were sighted bearing 340° and 360° respectively. These were later identified as two destroyers steering north-east. Range was about three nautical miles.
At 0451 A/6, HrMs Dolfijn dived and left patrol as per Capt. S 8's instructions in his signal timed 1238 A/5.

At 0525 A/6, a small destroyer was seen steering south-west and eventually passing about 2000 yards ahead. When it was getting lighter it was seen that it was not a destroyer but some sort of escort vessel looking much like a small destroyer but shorter and without guns aft.

9 Apr 1943 In a signal timed 0112 A/9, the Capt. S.8 sent the following orders to HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN); Pass position 38°00'N, 04°56'E at 2115 A/9 and make rendezvous with the escort to Algiers in position 36°59'N, 03°19'E at 0915 A/10 proceeding on the surface at your discretion on the 10th. (22)
10 Apr 1943 Around 1015 A/10, HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) ended her 6th war patrol (4th in the Mediterranean) at Algiers.
She was escorted in [identity of the escort not known]. The escort had joined around 0815 A/10. (9)

24 Apr 1943 Around 1845 A/24, HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) departed Algiers for her 7th war patrol (5th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol to the north of Sicily.
For the daily and attack positions of HrMs Dolfijn during this patrol see the map below. The map is however incomplete. No daily positions are currently known for 29 April to 3 May 1943.

26 Apr 1943 HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) attacked but missed the Italian submarine Dandolo with four torpedoes west-south-west of Sardinia. The Italian submarine spotted three torpedo tracks and turned away, two missed passed on her port side and the third on the starboard side.
0305 A/26 - In position 38°15'N, 05°52'E sighted a submarine steering a course of 340° abaft our beam. Range was between 2000 and 3000 yards.
0307 A/26 - The enemy zigged, course was now 320°.
0309 A/26 - Started attack.
0310 A/26 - Fired a salvo of four torpedoes from 1500 yards. Unfortunately, right at that moment the enemy started going astern and all torpedoes missed.
0312 A/26 - Dived. The enemy remained on the surface and made off to the northward.

In a signal timed 0450 A/26, which was passed around 0830 A/26 after an earlier attempt around 0515 A/26 had failed. This signal contained the following; After passing HMS Saracen (Lt. M.G.R. Lumby, DSO, DSC, RN) fired at an enemy submarine with four torpedoes, missed through enemy going astern, position 38°13'N, 06°05'E. Consider the remaining four torpedoes unsufficient for a full patrol, propose returning to replenish, proceeding onward awaiting reply.

In a reply timed 1134 A/26, which was received on board HrMs Dolfijn at 1755 A/26, the Capt. S.8 sent the following; Your 0450 A/26. Do not return Algiers, proceed in accordance with your patrol orders. (9)

27 Apr 1943 At 0040 A/27, the Capt. S.8's signal timed 2108 A/26 was received which contained the following orders for HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN); Onward routeing through position 38°15'N, 10°01'E at 0230 A/28 thence to zone 'B'. (23)

27 Apr 1943 In a signal timed 1438 A/27, the Capt. S.8's sent the following information to HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) and HMS Unshaken (Lt. J. Whitton, RN); Recent reports from submarines state flat calm weather and excellent A/S conditions prevailing in the Tyrrhenian Sea. Under such conditions Dolfijn and Unshaken are free to withdraw as necessary to zones 'D' and 'E' respectively. (24)
29 Apr 1943 At 0045 A/29, north of Cape St. Vito, Sicily, HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) sighted a hospital ship proceeding to the south-west.
This was possibly the hospital ship Aquileia (Italian, 9448 GRT, built 1914). She sailed from Messina at 0650 B/28 and arrived at Tunis at 2245 B/29.
At 0430 A/29, a motor torpedo boat was sighted near Cape St. Vito proceeding eastwards. [We have been unable to identify this vessel.]
At 1250 A/29, a tank landing ship was seen proceeding eastwards following the coastal route.
This was possibly the Italian MZ 703. She sailed at 1001 B/29 from Marsala for Palermo.
At 1420 A/29, a large motor torpedo boat and a small motor torpedo boat were sighted. Avoiding action was taken.
This were possibly VAS 207 and VAS 212 (both were the same size though) which sailed from Palermo at 1300 B/29 for Trapani. (9)

29 Apr 1943 In a signal timed 2144 A/29, the Capt. S.8 sent the following orders to HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) and HMS Unshaken (Lt. J. Whitton, RN); Unshaken and Dolfijn try and be in position 117° - 30 miles by 1100 A/30 and 170° - 15 miles by 1100 A/30 respectively both from focal point 'F'. Unshaken and Dolfijn resume patrol as previously ordered after 1500 A/30 and 1900 A/30 respectively. (24)

2 May 1943 At 1100 A/2, near Cape Gallo, Sicily, HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) sighted two small schooners sailing eastwards within one mile of the coast. They were not attacked. (9)

2 May 1943 In a signal timed 1148 A/2, the Capt. S.8 sent the following orders to the submarines mentioned; 1) HMS Tactician (Lt.Cdr. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN) leaving patrol P.M./6 through positions 41°40'N, 10°42'E, 39°25'N, 10°42'E, 38°39'N, 09°39'E at 0800 A/8, thence course 268°, speed of advance 120 miles a day. 2) HMS Trident (Lt. P.E. Newstead, RN) is not to enter Tactician's area until after dark/6. 3) HMS Unrivalled (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) after passing position 'P' at 1500 A/3 proceed through zone 'B' to operate in zones 'C', 'F' and 'E' east 14°00'E. 4) HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) operating in zone 'D' after dark/3 and leaving patrol through position 38°42'N, 11°30'E at 0001 A/5 thence course 268°, speed of advance 105 miles a day. (25)

3 May 1943 At 0640 A/3, just east of Palermo, Sicily, HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) sighted what is thought to be an Orsa-class torpedo boat proceeding towards Palermo along the coastal route. The enemy passed at a range of 4000 yards at 15 knots. She was sighted rather late due to the poor visibility. Due to the swell the torpedoes could not be set to six feet and an attack was thus not possible.

This was possibly the Italian torpedo boat Tifone. She sailed at 0030 B/3 from Messina and arrived at Trapani at 1200 B/3. It is not clear if she was escorting the transport Belluno (Italian, former French Fort de France, 4279 GRT, built 1935) at that time as she sailed with her from Trapani at 1900 B/3 for Tunis. As Tifone was lost during this passage not much info is available except a report of her sinking. (9)

4 May 1943 HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) intended to shell a schooner with the 3" gun at St. Maria Bay, Ustica. Unfortunately, the gun malfunctioned and not a single shot was lost.
Marina Messina reported a submarine sighting at 1115 B/4 in 120° - Ustica – 6 miles but did not mention the the identity of the vessel ' attacked '.

0650 A/4 - Sighted a Swedish Red Cross ship proceeding eastwards three nautical miles to the north of Ustica. Also sighted a schooner in St. Maria Bay. [We have been unable to identify this Red Cross ship.]
0850 A/4 - Having found no target on the north coast of Ustica set course for St. Maria Bay to deal with the schooner.
1020 A/4 - Surfaced to gun a 150-ton schooner in St. Maria Bay, Ustica. Unfortunately, the gun jammed and it was not possible to get it working.
1025 A/4 - Dived and retired from the area without firing a single shot.
1200 A/4 - Rounded Ustica and left patrol to return to Algiers. (9)

4 May 1943 In a signal timed 1408 A/4 the Capt. S.8 sent the following orders to the submarines mentioned; 1) My 1148 A/4. Onward routeing for HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) through positions 38°42'M, 11°30'E at 0001/5, 38°32'N, 03°58'E at 0800 A/8 thence make rendezvous with the escort to Algiers in position 36°57'N, 03°03'E at 0615 A/9. 2) Onward routeing for HMS Tactician (Lt.Cdr. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN) through position 38°39'N, 09°39'E at 0800 A/8, 38°32'N, 03°58'E at 2050 A/10 thence make rendezvous with the escort to Algiers in position 36°56'N, 03°10'E at 0615 A/11. (25)

7 May 1943 In a signal timed 2200 A/7, HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) informed the Capt. S.8 of the following; Am in position 38°25'N, 05°45'E. Maximum speed which can be maintained is 7 knots. Cannot make the rendezvous with the escort on time due to the heavy sea conditions. Request shortened route. Am proceeding as ordered awaiting reply.

In a signal timed 0238 A/8, the Capt. S.8 replied as follows; Your 2200 A/8 acknowledged. Proceed through position 38°23'N, 04°42'E at 0500 A/8 thence make rendezvous with the escort to Algiers in position 36°58'N, 03°12'E at 0700 A/9. (25)
9 May 1943 Around 1025 A/9, HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) ended her 7th war patrol (5th in the Mediterranean) at Algiers.
She was escorted in [name of the escort not known] which had joined around 0820 A/9. (9)

20 May 1943 Around 1500 A/20, HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) departed Algiers for her 8th war patrol (6th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol to the north of the Straits of Messina south of 39°30'N and east of 14°00'E.
The submarine was routed through positions 38°07'N, 04°40'E at 0445 A/21 and 38°45'N, 11°30'E at 2130 A/24 thence through zones 'A' and 'B' to her patrol area.
For the daily and attack positions of HrMs Dolfijn during this patrol see the map below. The map is however incomplete. No daily positions are currently known for 1 to 4 June 1943.
25 May 1943 HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) damaged the Italian patrol vessel M 9 with gunfire off Ustica.

1630 B/25 - While passing Ustica investigated Santa Maria Anchorage. A small tug of about 200 tons and an apparently unarmed small M.T.B., no depth charges being visible and no torpedo on the starboard side. In order to make sure no torpedo was carried on the port side Dolfijn closed. As the M.T.B. was moored at a very sharp angle to the coast, Dolfijn had to come very close inshore. A torpedo was now seen to be carried on the port side so the plan for gun action was abandoned.
1700 B/25 - Lt.Cdr. van Oostrom Soede had overestimated the distance to the coast and Dolfijn grounded at slow speed on the rocks. While getting off Dolfijn came to 20 feet.

1702 B/25 - Blew main tanks; manned the machine gun and the 3" gun to keep the M.T.B. at a distance in case we could not get off right away. Kept the M.T.B. covered with machine gun fire, doing damage. A machine gun on the shore had meanwhile opened fire on Dolfijn.
1705 B/25- Dived as Dolfijn was now in deeper water.
1706 B/25 - Periscope depth. It was now reported that there were two wounded.

In a signal timed 2105 B/25, HrMs Dolfijn informed the Capt. S.8 of the following; At Ustica 3rd Officer and one rating wounded by machine gun bullets. Rating leg wound not serious. Officer bullet in back above hip bone probably glancing hit. Bullet still inside. Kidneys and intestines possibly not affected. Intend to proceed through position 38°40'N, 12°00'E at 0500 B/26 diving by day to position 37°30'N, 09°32'E and then to Bizerta. Speed of advance 95 miles per day. Propose wounded if possible be transferred to ship carrying doctor, afterwards resume patrol, request instructions.

In a signal timed 0020 B/26 the Capt. S.8 replied as follows; Your 2105 B/25. Proceed to pass position 'P' at 2200 B/26 thence to Malta as ordered by the Capt. S.10 to transfer the wounded. HMS Tactician (Lt.Cdr. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN) passes position 'P' at 0001 B/27, speed of advance 100 miles per day. Dress office r's wound with Field dressings, keep patient still and do not attempt to remove bullet.

In a signal timed 0418 B/26, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders; Route from position 'P'. Proceed through position 37°07'N, 15°15'E thence to make rendezvous with a minesweeper in position 36°08'N, 12°22'E at 0830 B/28. HMS Tactician is also due at this time. (27)
25 May 1943 In a signal timed 1536 B/25, the Capt. S.8 sent the following orders to HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN); Patrol in zone 'F' after 0001 B/28. (28)

27 May 1943 At 0300 B/27, HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) sighted what is thought to be a motor torpedo boat very close. Nothing head been heard on the Asdic. HrMs Dolfijn quickly dived to evade.
Twenty minutes later all was clear and HrMs Dolfijn surfaced to continue her passage to Malta.
This however must have been HMS Unruffled (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSO, DSC, RN) which reported seeing a submarine at the same time. (9)
28 May 1943 Around 1115 B/28, HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) arrived at Malta to land her two wounded crewmembers.
She, and HMS Tactician (Lt.Cdr. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN) were escorted in [name of the escort not known]. The escort had joined around 0900 B/28. (9)
29 May 1943 Around 1700 B/29, HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) departed Malta to resume her patrol.
She was orders to proceed through positions 'S' and 'P'. The patrol area would be signalled by the Capt. S.8. (9)

30 May 1943 In a signal timed 1114 B/30, the Capt. S.8 sent the following orders to HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN); After passing position 'P' at about 1200 B/31 proceed through zones 'A' and 'B' to operate in zones 'C' and 'E' east of 14°00'E. (28)

1 Jun 1943 At 1320 B/1, off Cape Zafferano, near Palermo, Sicily, HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) sighted a Generale class torpedo boat escorting two vessels toward Palermo. The submarine closed to attack but this was later broken off when the two vessels proved to be a small armed tug and another very small vessel which kept very close inshore.
These ships were most probably the torpedo boat Generale Achille Abba escorting the tugs Titano and Ursus to Palermo where they arrived about an hour later.
At 1345 B/1, the vessels signalled with Cape Zafferano and then turned round apparently searching until 1400 B/1, when they made off for Palermo.
At 1415 B/1, an Orsa or Climene class torpedo boat approached from the eastward searching on Asdic. She searched near Cape Zafferano for 15 minutes and then made off for Palermo.
At 1609 B/1, a signal was received from Capt. S 8 (Capt S 8's 1502 B/1) that a submarine had been sighted off Cape Zafferano, this explains the, half-hearted, searches by the Italians.

The torpedo sighted was the Orione. She had sailed from Messina at 0508 B/1, escorting the motor vessel Genepesca to Milazzo and then continued alone to Palermo where she arrived at 1522 B/1. At 1330 B/1, her Metox signalled a submarine and at 1345 B/1 she arrived off Cape Zafferano where she got a signal that an air raid was in progress over Palermo and she delayed her arrival. [The ' Genepesca ' could be either the Genepesca I (Italian, 1600 GRT, built 1940) or Genepesca II (Italian, 1628 GRT, built 1940)].

3 Jun 1943 At 1720 B/3, off Lipari, HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) sighted a small schooner and an armed patrol vessel of about 200 tons. Both soon disappeared behind the breakwater before there was a chance for a gun action. (9)

4 Jun 1943 HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) left patrol to return to Algiers in accordance with the Capt. S.8's signal timed 1342 B/4 [or 1352 B/4, sources give two different times, one of which is probably a typo.] which had contained the following orders; Leave zone 'C' on the 4th so as to pass through positions 38°25'N, 11°30'E at 2100 B/5, 38°08'N, 03°42'E at 1800 B/9 thence to make rendezvous with the escort to Algiers in position 36°56'N, 03°10'E at 0630 B/10. (9)

9 Jun 1943 In a signal timed 0938 B/9, the Capt. S.8 sent the following orders / information to HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) and HMS Seraph (Lt. N.L.A. Jewell, OBE, RN); Your rendezvous tomorrow will be in position 37°00'N, 03°16'E at 0600 B/10th.

In a signal timed 1414 B/9, the Capt. S.8 informed the same submarines of the following; 1) HMS Coriolanus (T/Lt. N. Hunt, RNVR) and HMML 460 (T/S.Lt. K.D. Dewar, RNVR) will meet you in position 37°00'N, 03°16'E at 0600 B/10. The first arrival will be escorted to harbour by ML 460, the second by Coriolanus. 2) A large eastbound convoy will be passing Algiers around 0700 B/10. 3) ML's are patrolling east and west of Algiers. (29)
10 Jun 1943 Around 0835 B/10, HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) ended her 8th war patrol (6th in the Mediterranean) at Algiers.

She was escorted in. The escort had joined around 0615 A/10. Most likely the escort was HMS Coriolanus (T/Lt. N. Hunt, RNVR) as HrMs Dolfijn had arrived second (see the 'event' for the previous day). (9)

27 Jun 1943 Around 1945 B/27, HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) departed Algiers for her 9th war patrol (7th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol between Giglio Island and the Italian mainland to provide cover during Operation Husky.
HrMs Dolfijn was routed through positions 38°00'N, 05°00'E at 2230 B/28 and 38°30'N, 10°00'E at 1430 B/1 after which she was to steer a course of 028°.
For the daily and attack positions of HrMs Dolfijn during this patrol see the map below. The map is however incomplete. No daily positions are currently known for 3 to 13 July 1943.

29 Jun 1943 Around 1345 B/29, HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) passed the Swedish Red Cross ships Bardaland (Swedish, 2956 GRT, built 1936) and Camelia (Swedish, 1688 GRT, built 1941) proceeding westwards. (9)

4 Jul 1943 HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) torpedoed and damaged the Italian transport Sabbia (5788 GRT, built 1926) south of Civitavecchia, Italy in position 42°05'N, 11°47'E. Sabbia was escorted by the German auxiliary submarine chaser UJ 2207 which also towed the damaged ship into Civitavecchia where she was beached, partly blocking the entrance.
Sabbia and UJ 2207 had departed Naples around 1300 B/3 for Santa Stefano. Shortly before the torpedo hit a periscope had been sighted by UJ 2207.

Also on this day HrMs Dolfijn sank the Italian auxiliary patrol vessel V 50 / Adalia (165 GRT) with gunfire about 15 nautical miles west of Civitavecchia, Italy in position 42°02'N, 11°20'E. The crew escaped in a lifeboat and reached the coast at Torre Chiaruccia the following morning.
0632 B/4 - In position 41°44'N, 11°52'E sighted a 5000-6000 ton merchant vessel with one escort at a range of about 7000 yards. Started attack.

0709 B/4 - Fired two torpedoes from 400 yards. One hit was obtained. Dolfijn took avoiding action and went deep. Depth control was lost and Dolfijn ended up at 300 feet but was soon able to go to 200 feet.
0731 B/4 - A counter attack in which a pattern of 6 depth charges was dropped, quite close. [Actually a pattern of 12 depth charges was dropped by UJ 2207.]
0745 B/4 - Another pattern of 9 depth charges. Again quite close. The depth charging caused some minor damage. [Actually another pattern of 12 depth charges was dropped by UJ 2207.]
0930 B/4 - Came to periscope depth. The target was about 5 nautical miles to the north-east. She was very much down by the stern and it appeared she would sink soon.
0957 B/4 - It was seen that the escort had taken the ship in tow. [Meanwhile the motor minesweeper R 8 had joined as had an Italian patrol vessel. Later two Italian tug took over the tow.]
2000 B/4 - Sighted a three-masted armed patrol schooner. Remained nearby for gun action.
2110 B/4 - Surfaced for gun action in position 42°02'N, 11°20'E. Opened fire from 3500 yards. The crew abandoned ship in a dinghy.
2116 B/4 - Stopped firing after 43 rounds for 8 hits. But the gun was not functioning properly.
2125 B/4 - Boarded the schooner. Collected logbooks and papers and placed demolition charges.
2215 B/4 - The charges exploded and set the ship on fire.
2250 B/4 - The schooner blew up and sank. (31)

13 Jul 1943 HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) sank the Italian auxiliary patrol vessel V 105 / Stefano Galleano (137 GRT) with gunfire off Ostia, Italy in position 41°41'N, 12°00'E. One of the crew was killed, two were missing and three were wounded.

0545 B/13 - While patrolling off Civitavecchia a 3000 tons merchant vessel, in ballast, leaving the harbour. She proceeded to the south-east. Range was about 4 nautical miles and it was opening rapidly so an attack was not possible. The harbour was then examined and three merchant vessels of about 3000 to 6000 tons were seen inside it.

1630 B/13 - Sighted a camouflaged armed yacht of about 1000 - 1500 tons, escorted by a Cant Z.501 aircraft. Started an attack. During the attack about 40 aircraft passed very close. The range could not be closed sufficiently and the attack had to be broken off.
2134 B/13 - Surfaced in position 41°41'N, 12°00'E for gun action with an armed patrol schooner. Range was 2500 yards. 23 rounds were fired. All were hits except for one near-miss.
2200 B/13 - Boarded the schooner and placed explosive charges. Found one of the crew killed. The others had abandoned ship in their dinghy.
2225 B/13 - After burning fiercely the schooner sank. (9)

14 Jul 1943 HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) left patrol to return to Algiers in accordance with the orders contained in the Capt. S.8's signal timed 1610 B/12 which had contained the following orders for Dolfijn; You are to leave patrol passing through positions 41°00'N, 11°48'E at 0800 B/14, 38°27'N, 09°59'E at 1000 B/16, 38°01'N, 03°59'E at 2200 B/19, thence make rendezvous with the escort to Algiers in position 36°58'N, 03°14'E at 0645 B/20. (9)
20 Jul 1943 Around 0845 B/20, HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) ended her 9th war patrol (7th in the Mediterranean) at Algiers.
She was escorted in [name of the escort not known to us at the moment]. The escort had joined around 0635 A/20. (9)
24 Jul 1943 HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) is docked in the large graving dock at Algiers. (32)
1 Aug 1943 HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) is undocked. (32)

4 Aug 1943 Around 1920 B/4, HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) departed Algiers for her 10th war patrol (8th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off Toulon, Southern France, north of 42°20'N and between 05°00'E and 07°00'E.
She was routed through positions 38°28'N, 05°16'E at 1330 B/5, 40°36'N, 05°27'E at 0530 B/6, thence diving by day through position 42°20'N, 05°38'E at 1100 B/7.
For the daily and attack positions of HrMs Dolfijn during this patrol see the map below. The map is however incomplete. No daily positions are currently known for 8 to 11 and 13 to 15 August 1943.

11 Aug 1943 At 0745 B/11, while patrolling off Toulon, HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) sighted three motor torpedo boats patrolling from west to east at a range of two nautical miles.

0745 B/11: could this be the German auxiliary submarine chaser UJ 6071 and the German auxiliary minesweepers M 7607 and M 7608. The description does not seem to match though. These ships had departed Toulon around 0600 B/11 for A/S operations and escort duties for two ships coming from Corsica. [These two ships did not depart so the escort duties were cancelled.]
At 0830 B/11, three minesweepers were sighted leaving Toulon but they turned back an hour later.
This were probably the German auxiliary minesweepers M 6001, M 6003 and M 6005 which escorted the German submarine U-371 in that morning.

At 1720 A/11, Dolfijn received the Capt. S.8's signal timed 1124 B/11 which contained the following orders; Try and be in position 74° - focal point 'H' - 26 miles by 1900 B/11. Resume normal patrol after 1200 B/12.
In a signal timed 1126 B/11 the time was corrected to 2130 B/11.
This positioning was to try to intercept the German submarine U-380 which was due to leave Toulon for patrol. This was known to the Allies due to ULTRA intercepts of the enemy communication. (34)

12 Aug 1943 At 0010 B/12, HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN), sighted a southbound U-boat in position 42°50'N, 05°55'E. This was U-380 that had left Toulon for her patrol area around 1800 B/12. It was not possible to attack. U-380 had also sighted Dolfijn, which was up moon, but had orders not to attack submarines in this area as these may be friendly.

At 0555 B/12, near Port Cros Island, to the east of Toulon, two small vessels were sighted. This were thought to be a minesweeper and a large motor torpedo boat. Light was very poor. At 0620 B/12, as light improved, it was seen the the vessels were slightly larger than first thought. They were now about 5 nautical miles away and too far off to be attacked. The vessels were estimated to be around 700 to 800 tons.

The German auxiliary minelayers Brandenburg (former French Kita, 3895 GRT, built 1937) and Pommern (former French Belain d’Esnambuc, 2956 GRT, built 1940) arrived at Toulon around 0830 B/12. Could this perhaps have been the ships sighted ? The discription does not really match but light conditions were difficult. Also the course and speed of the enemy vessels are not given in the patrol report and log of HrMs Dolfijn. However the same ships were seen around 2025 B/14 which coincide with them leaving Toulon.

At 2143 B/12, HrMs Dolfijn, sighted another southbound U-boat in position 42°40'N, 05°55'E. This was most likely U-431 that had left Toulon for her patrol area around 1400 B/12. Again it was not possible to attack. The Germans had not seen HrMs Dolfijn.

U-380 had departed Toulon around 1400 B/12 escorted until 1545 B/12 by the German auxiliary minesweepers M 6001, M 6003 and M 6005. These auxiliary minesweepers returned to Toulon around 1700 B/12. (35)
14 Aug 1943 At 1945 B/14, a motor torpedo boat was seen leaving Toulon and proceeding westwards.

At 2025 B/14, [the patrol report gives 1800 B/14 which is an error, the log gives 2025 B/14] HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN), sighted two small merchant vessels leaving Toulon to the eastwards. As HrMs Dolfijn was at that moment at the extreme western end of her patrol area, the enemy vessels were well out of range.
They were thought to be a KT-class transport and possibly the small merchant vessel Duino (1334 GRT, built 1923). [As Duino had been mined and sunk off Bari on 8 February 1942 this was not possible.]
It is now also thought this were the same ships that had been sighted at 0555 B/12.

This were most likely the German auxiliary minelayers Brandenburg (former French Kita, 3895 GRT, built 1937) and Pommern (former French Belain d’Esnambuc, 2956 GRT, built 1940) which had departed Toulon for La Maddalena around 2000 B/14. (34)

16 Aug 1943 HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) left patrol to return to Algiers in accordance with the Capt. S.8's signal timed 1242 B/14 which had contained the following orders; Leave patrol through positions 42°20'N, 05°46'E at 1200 B/16, 40°37'N, 05°30'E at 2215 B/17 thence on the surface at the Commanding Officers discretion through position 39°02'N, 05°16'E at 1015 B/18 to make rendezvous with the escort to Algiers in position 36°57'N, 03°07'E at 0630 B/19. (36)

18 Aug 1943 In a signal timed 1902 B/18, the Capt. S.8 sent the following information / orders to HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN); 1) Your escort will be HMS St. Kilda (A/Skr.Lt. R.G. Utting, RNR). 2) Set additional watch on 4900 kc/s per sec. from 0500 B/19. Morning fog may be expected. 3) Two destroyers from the eastward will be arriving about 0400 B/19. 4) One Fleet Unit and four destroyers will be passing Algiers eastbound at about 0800/19th. 5) The usual ML patrol may be expected east and west of Algiers.
[We have been unable to identify the destroyer referred to in paragraph 3.]

[Paragraph 4 must have referred to the aircraft carrier HMS Illustrious (Capt. R.L.B. Cunliffe, RN) on passage from Gibraltar to Malta escorted by the escort destroyers HMS Hurworth (Cdr. R.H. Wright, DSC, RN), HMS Blankney (Lt.Cdr. D.H.R. Bromley, RN), HMS Croome (Lt. H.D.M. Slater, RN) and RHS Adrias (?). Off Algiers, HMS Blankney was relieved by RHS Kanaris (?).

In a signal timed 0200 B/19, the Capt. S.8 sent the following information to HrMs Dolfijn; A Wellington aircraft is searching for a suspected enemy submarine south of 37°00'N, between 02°40'E and 03°20'E until 0200 A/19.
[We can't place an enemy submarine in the Algiers area on this day.] (37)
19 Aug 1943 Around 0730 B/19, HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) ended her 10th war patrol (8th in the Mediterranean) at Algiers.
She was escorted in, most likely by HMS St. Kilda (A/Skr.Lt. R.G. Utting, RNR). The escort had joined around 0610 B/19. (9)

3 Sep 1943 Around 1200 B/3, HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) departed Algiers for her 11th war patrol (9th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol between Corsica and the Italian mainland between latitudes 41°30'N / 43°05'N and longitudes 10°01'E / 10°40'E.
For the daily and attack positions of HrMs Dolfijn during this patrol see the map below. The map is however incomplete. No daily positions are currently known for 9, 11, 14 and 14 September 1943.

7 Sep 1943 In a signal timed 1024 B/7, the Capt. S.8 sent the following orders to the submarines listed; HMS Sportsman (Lt. R. Gatehouse, DSC, RN), HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) and HMS Seraph (Lt. N.L.A. Jewell, OBE, RN) in areas 'SB', 'SC' and 'SD' respectively are to keep north of 42°01'N where practicable after 0600 B/8. It is realised that Dolfijn may not be north of 42°01'N until P.M./8. (39)

8 Sep 1943 Around 1525 B/8, east of Corsica, HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) started an attack on a large merchant vessel. The attack had to be broken off when this turned out to be a hospital ship which was proceeding westwards at slow speed (about 5 knots).

This was the Gariglia (Italian, ) which was positively identified by Lt.Cdr. van Oostrom Soede. According to Italian records this ship arrived at Bonifacio at 1445 B/8 which must be a mistake. She therefore certainly in the area. (9)
8 Sep 1943 In a signal timed 0956 B/8 from the C-in-C, Mediterranean all submarines received the following orders; Until further orders enemy cruisers and destroyers are not to be attacked.

Submarines at sea in the western Mediterranean at this moment were HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN), HMS Universal (Lt. C. Gordon, RN), HMS Seraph (Lt. N.L.A. Jewell, OBE, RN), HMS Shakespeare (Lt. M.F.R. Ainslie, DSC, RN), HMS Simoom (Lt. G.D.N. Milner, DSC, RN) and HMS Sportsman (Lt. R. Gatehouse, DSC, RN).

In a signal timed 2048 B/8, the C-in-C, Mediterranean informed all vessels in the Mediterranean of the following; An armistice with Italy has been declared. Operations now in progression are to proceed as ordered but Italian Armed Forces including aircraft should be treated as friendly unless they take hostile action or threaten hostile action.

In a signal timed 0210 B/9, the Capt. S.8 informed the submarines of the following; Following is substance of instructions given to Italian Authorities. A) All Italian Naval ships to sail as soon as possible after dark, and if possible in groups and proceed at their highest speed as follows. B) Warships north of 42°00'N to proceed north and west of Corsica and west of Sardinia to Bone. C) Warships south of 42°00'N to proceed north hugging west coast Italy as far as 42°00'N thence due west to Corsica thence south hugging the east coast of Corsica and Sardinia thence to Bone. D) Submarines in harbour to sail by same route as surface vessels proceeding on surface if possible with escort. E) Submarines at sea to proceed on the surface to nearest of following ports - Gibraltar, Palermo of Malta. F) Merchant ships west of 17°00'E and north of 42°00'N to proceed north of Corsica or through Straits of Bonifacio thence direct to Gibraltar but may call at Bone or Algiers for fuel. G) All Italian Naval or merchant ships to fly a large black or blue pendant from the main-mast by day and to burn dimmed navigation lights and flash letters GA if challenged by night. H) United Nations ports to be approached in full daylight at less than 12 knots. (39)

9 Sep 1943 In a signal timed 1242 B/9. the Capt. S.8 sent the following orders to the submarines stated; 1) HMS Universal (Lt. C. Gordon, RN) proceed forthwith to patrol between 05°30'E and 06°30'E and north of 42°30'N. Object German U-Boats and German shipping. 2) After sunset today, Thursday, HMS Sportsman (Lt. R. Gatehouse, DSC, RN) and HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) proceed to areas '3A' and '3B' respectively and act in accordance with my signal timed 0100 B/9 [we have been unable to find the text of this signal]. (39)
10 Sep 1943 At 0105 B/10, HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN), encountered the Italian minelaying submarine Filippo Corridoni in position 42°45'N, 09°54'E.

A conference was then held on board Dolfijn. Corridoni had escaped from La Maddalena when the port was taken over by the Germans. She had been refitting at La Maddalena and was unable to dive. Also she had no lubricating oil on board. A signal was now sent to the Capt S.8 requesting orders. After a few hours these orders were received and passed to the Corridoni. The submarines then parted company.

The signal sent by HrMs Dolfijn was timed 0236 B/10 and contained the following; Have intercepted Italian cargo submarine Corridoni. Escaped from Maddalena making for an Allied port. Has no oil to do so. Proposes to enter Elba to fuel and request orders. Am keeping her with me awaiting orders if possible before daylight.

The response by the Capt. S.8, timed 0540 B/10 was as follows; Your 0236 B/10. Yes. Thence to vicinity east coast Sardinia thence to Bone reporting her position, course and speed when south of Sardinia. Many other Italian submarines are on their way to join the Allies. At your discretion to contact the Italian submarine after she leaves Elba and then resume patrol.
Corridoni eventually put into Palermo on 13 September 1943 after having embarked lubricating oil at Porto Ferraio, Elba during 10/11 September. (9)
11 Sep 1943 HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) torpedoed and damaged the Italian transport Humanitas (7980 GRT, built 1943) off Bastia, Corsica.

The Naval Command in Bastia ordered the tug Turbine to tow Humanitas to Porto Ferraio. However, as she was coming out of the harbour, she was bombed and damaged by German aircraft and had to return to harbour. The towing operation was cancelled and the corvettes Gabbiano and Pellicano finished off the ship with gunfire, provoking a violent explosion at 2326 B/11.

0850 B/11 - A large landing craft under the German flag thought to be displaying number F 584, entered Bastia unmolested. [F 584 was serving in the Black Sea so it could not have been this one. It must have been F 554 which arrived off Bastia around 0900 B/11 but the harbour defence boom was not opened by the Italians and she subsequently set course for Livorno / Leghorn.] It was until then thought that Bastia was in friendly hands so this seemed strange.
0950 B/11 - The landing craft departed Bastia again and proceeded in the direction of Elba. Lt.Cdr. van Oostrom Soede was now convinced that Bastia was in German hands.

1515 B/11 - Two Italian torpedo boats, thought to be a Climene-class and a Partenope-class approached Bastia from the north-eastward. They were not flying the black flag and were not passing north and west of Corsica as they should have done so Lt.Cdr. van Oostrom Soede considered they were under enemy control. They waited close outside Bastia harbour.

1542 B/11 - Sighted a large merchant vessel about 1 nautical mile outside Bastia harbour. She had just left this harbour. Enemy course was 075°. The two torpedo boats were escorting the merchant ship. Started attack.
1548 B/11 - Fired a full salvo of four torpedoes.

1550 B/11 - One torpedo hit the stern of the enemy doing tremendous damage and nearly blowing the stern off. No counter attack followed although the torpedo boats were searching. Dolfijn stayed at periscope depth and took avoiding action as necessary.
1630 B/11 - Sighted a second merchant vessel leaving Bastia harbour and proceeding southwards. It was not displaying a black flag.

1715 B/11 - The torpedo boats were well clear and a second attempt was made to try to finish off the heavily damaged transport.
